mainText:
If you only remember one thing today, make it this. The model Anthropic spent months calling too dangerous to release went public last week as Fable Five. And today, the U S government forced Anthropic to switch it off for foreign users. The safety debate just became an export-control story.

Story one. Anthropic's too-dangerous model gets switched off. Quick recap, because this is the thread of the week. Back in April, Anthropic said its most powerful model, called Mythos, was too dangerous to release. It pointed to cyber, biology, and chemistry risks. Last week, it changed course and shipped a guard-railed public version called Fable Five. That version blocks high-risk questions and falls back to a safer model. Then today, Anthropic disabled both Fable and Mythos for foreign access, after the U S government deemed broad availability a national-security threat. Your Reads caught the whole arc. Swyx's A I News led with, Fable and Mythos officially too dangerous to release. The A I Collective ran, Anthropic held it back in April, now a version is public. And The Batch framed it as, Mythos begets Fable. Why it matters: a private company's safety call just collided with federal policy. It's the clearest sign yet that frontier-model access is now a government decision, not just a corporate one.

Story two. Bezos bets big on an artificial general engineer. From your Rundown: Jeff Bezos is backing a reported forty-one billion dollar effort to build what they're calling an artificial general engineer. This is A I aimed not at chatting, but at actually doing engineering work from start to finish. It's part of a bigger shift your newsletters keep flagging: agents that do tasks, rather than just answer questions. Why it matters: the money is moving from chatbots to autonomous workers. And that reshapes which jobs A I touches first.

Story three. Europe sets the rules for A I-generated content. On policy: the European Commission published its Code of Practice on marking and labeling A I-generated content on June tenth. It sits alongside the broader A I Act deal from May, which extends some deadlines but keeps the full A I Act landing on August second. The transparency rules, the this-was-made-by-A-I labels, are part of that wave. Why it matters: if you work in comms, labeling and disclosure rules are about to become a daily practical question, not a future one.

Story four. Taiwan tightens the chip screws. Geopolitics: Taiwan is weighing much stricter export controls on A I chips going to China, to line up with U S measures. The goal is closing the gap that lets advanced A I servers get diverted to Beijing. Why it matters: the chip chokepoint is widening from Washington to Taipei. And Taiwan makes the chips everyone is fighting over.

Story five. The race for data centers in space. From your RecodeChina A I Reads: the contest to put data centers in orbit has narrowed to a U S versus China race. It's being pitched as China's answer to SpaceX. It's still early, and still speculative, but it's a real signal of where computing power and geopolitics are heading. Why it matters: if computing moves off the planet, the export-control and energy debates follow it up there.

And to close, some good news. Purpose-built A I agents from two groups, Signpost and aprendIA, are now helping displaced people in Bangladesh and Nigeria get access to food, education, and legal documents. In Nigeria, aprendIA went from a small pilot to reaching four thousand seven hundred teachers, and it's on track for twenty-two thousand by year's end. It's built for low bandwidth, local languages, and offline use. Why it matters: the same models making headlines for risk are quietly doing real humanitarian work, in places where funding is thin.

Also in your Reads today: Noosphere on Middle East negotiations with analyst Aaron David Miller. James Clear's three-two-one on improving the world and feeling wealthy. Galleries Now's weekend exhibition picks across Europe and the U S. And Harvard Online's free summer series on omens and oracles.

One quick note: your Readwise saves weren't reachable this morning, so today's brief runs on your inbox Reads plus live web search.

The thread today: every big story, the disabled model, the chip curbs, the new labels, is really one story. Who gets to decide what A I can do, and where. That's it for this morning. Have a great Saturday, Sin.
